response.prompt: View the chart on the right and write your best caption for it in the box below it. Then, read the expert and AI captions and decide which you think is the best.
Which caption do you like the most?

parameters.ai-caption: Global plastic production grew from virtually nothing in 1950 to roughly 460 million tonnes by 2019 — a nearly exponential rise interrupted only briefly by the 1973 oil crisis and the 2008 financial crisis — with no signs of leveling off despite growing environmental concern.
parameters.expert-caption: Total annual plastic production is global annual pure polymer (resin) production data from 1950 to 2015, published by the Plastics Europe Market Research Group, and global annual fiber production data from 1970 to 2015 published by The Fiber Year and Tecnon OrbiChem (Geyer et al. 2017)

parameters.ai-caption: Aquaculture production in 2023 is overwhelmingly concentrated in Asia, with China alone accounting for an outsized share of global farmed fish output — dwarfing production in every other region — while most of Africa and parts of the Americas and Middle East produce very little farmed seafood.
parameters.expert-caption: Most of the world’s growth in aquaculture production has come from East Asia and the Pacific. For example, China produces more food from aquaculture than any other country.

parameters.ai-caption: Transport CO₂ emissions in 2023 are heavily concentrated in the United States and China, which appear as the darkest regions on the map, while most of Africa and parts of South and Southeast Asia generate comparatively little transport-related carbon — reflecting stark differences in vehicle ownership, infrastructure, and economic activity.
parameters.expert-caption: Emissions are measured in tonnes. Domestic aviation and shipping emissions are included at the national level.International aviation and shipping emissions are included only at the global level.

parameters.ai-caption: In 2025, population growth is starkly divided along geographic lines: sub-Saharan Africa is growing fastest, with several countries exceeding 2–3% annually, while Russia, Eastern Europe, and parts of East Asia are experiencing natural population decline, reflecting a world where demographic trajectories are pulling in opposite directions.
parameters.expert-caption: Natural population growth is the population increase determined by births and deaths. Migration flows are not taken into account. Future projections are based on the UN medium scenario.

parameters.ai-caption: Protein sources vary dramatically by country and income level: Nigeria and India draw 89% and 75% of their protein from plants respectively, while the United States gets 41% from meat and Iceland gets 23% from dairy, reflecting deep differences in diet, culture, and agricultural systems.
parameters.expert-caption: Globally, less than 10% of total protein intake comes from seafood. In some countries, it accounts for a much larger share: around one-third in the Maldives and one-fifth in Japan, Iceland, and Cambodia.

parameters.ai-caption: Since the first detection in 2015, scientists have cumulatively observed over 220 gravitational wave events by 2024, with the rate of new detections accelerating sharply after 2017 as detector sensitivity improved, transforming gravitational wave astronomy from a curiosity into a prolific observational science.
parameters.expert-caption: Gravitational waves are ripples in the fabric of space-time that are created when two massive objects, such as black holes or neutron stars, collide or interact. They move at the speed of light, but they are extremely difficult to detect because they are so small and faint. It wasn't until the development of advanced technology like LIGO (the Laser Interferometer Gravitational-Wave Observatory) that researchers were able to detect these waves for the first time in 2015.

parameters.ai-caption: The number of objects launched into space remained roughly stable at under 200 per year from the late 1950s through the 2010s, before exploding to nearly 4,000 in 2025 — a surge driven almost entirely by commercial satellite mega-constellations such as Starlink.
parameters.expert-caption: As our presence in space has increased, so has the issue of pollution. Our many launches into space have created debris, including abandoned rocket stages, old satellites, and other discarded equipment. This debris poses a significant risk to future space exploration, as it can collide with and damage functioning satellites or even endanger astronauts on space missions. This is an ongoing challenge that will require continued research and innovation to solve.

parameters.ai-caption: As of 2024, the world has 88 electoral democracies, with the largest cohort (31 countries) aged 31–60 years, while 91 countries remain under some form of autocratic rule — 60 electoral autocracies and 31 closed autocracies.
parameters.expert-caption: Only half of all countries were democracies in 2024: of the 179 countries for which data is available, 88 held meaningful, free and fair, and multi-party elections.

parameters.ai-caption: Global life expectancy in 2023 varies dramatically by region, with Europeans living an average of 79.1 years compared to just 63.8 years in Africa — a gap of more than 15 years.
parameters.expert-caption: Period life expectancy is an indicator that summarizes death rates across all age groups in one particular year. It shows how long the average baby born in that year would be expected to live if they experienced the same chances of dying at each age as people did in that year.

parameters.ai-caption: The United States emits 14.2 tonnes of CO₂ per person annually — nearly three times the global average of 4.73 tonnes and more than double the EU's 5.39 tonnes — while the UK sits just below the world average at 4.53 tonnes.
parameters.expert-caption: CO₂ emissions per capita are calculated by dividing emissions by population. They represent the average emissions per person in a country or region.

parameters.ai-caption: China leads the world in fertilizer intensity, applying 336 kg per hectare of cropland in 2022 — three times the global average of 113 kg — driven largely by heavy nitrogen use, while African nations like Ghana and Nigeria use a fraction of that amount.
parameters.expert-caption: In many of the world’s poorest countries – particularly across Sub-Saharan Africa – farmers apply only a few kilograms of fertilizer per hectare. For context, one hectare is about 1.5-times the size of a football pitch.4 Contrast this with countries such as China, Brazil, the UK or Egypt, where farmers apply hundreds of kilograms per year. They apply as much in a few days as some farmers do in an entire year.

parameters.ai-caption: Lamb and mutton are the most carbon-intensive foods at 40 kg CO₂-equivalent per kilogram — even more than beef — with farm-stage emissions dominating, while plant-based foods like wheat, berries, and tomatoes generate less than 2 kg per kilogram, making the choice between animal and plant proteins one of the single largest levers available to individuals seeking to reduce their dietary carbon footprint.
parameters.expert-caption: Overall, animal-based foods tend to have a higher footprint than plant-based. Lamb and cheese both emit more than 20 kilograms of CO2-equivalents per kilogram. Poultry and pork have lower footprints but are still higher than most plant-based foods, at 6 and 7 kg CO2-equivalents, respectively.

parameters.ai-caption: Meat and fish consumption varies enormously across countries, with Portugal leading at 151 kg per person annually — driven largely by fish and seafood — while the US consumes 144 kg dominated by poultry and beef, compared to just 7 kg in Ethiopia where animal protein is a rare dietary luxury.
parameters.expert-caption: Consumption trends vary significantly across the world. In China, pigmeat accounts for around two-thirds of per capita meat consumption. In Argentina, beef and buffalo meat dominate, accounting for more than half of consumption. New Zealanders have a much stronger preference for mutton & goat meat relative to the global average.
